speaker: interviewer
text: November 27, 1996. The survivor is Robert Waisman-- W-A-I-S-M-A-N. Name at birth, Wajsman-- W-A-J-S-M-A-N. The interviewer is Fran Starr-- S-T-A-R-R. We are in the city of Vancouver, Canada. And the language will be in English. My name is Fran Starr. Today is November 27, 1996. I will be interviewing Robert Waisman. We are in the city of Vancouver, Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did you know that the best speeches are made on the way home?

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us your full name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Robert Waisman-- W-A-I-S-M-A-N. Was born with W-A-J-S-M-A-N. But when I came to Canada, it didn't make sense to have the J, so I took the J and put an I instead.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on February the 2nd, 1931, in Skarzysko-Kamienna. It's in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are the names of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Chil. And my mother's name was Rivka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had four brothers and one sister. In order of age, my oldest brother was Chaim. We used to call him Chamu. My next brother was Motel, then Moishe, and then Abram. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I was the baby of the family. And did you have a sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my sister Leah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do for a living, to support this family?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in the haberdashery business, and he was a tailor, in addition to, uh-- it sort of went hand in hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother work, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My mum looked after all of us, and that was a big job in itself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the street that you lived on, as a child growing up in Skarzysko?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we lived on Trzeciego Maja. The number was 69 Trzeciego Maja.

speaker: interviewer
text: What can you--

speaker: interviewee
text: The translation was 'the 3rd of May.' That was the name of the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: What can you tell me about your neighborhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: The neighborhood? It was just-- it was a wonderful neighborhood, you know, looking at it from a child's point of view. There were several Jews, of course, but we lived also among Gentiles. Some of my best friends-- one was Diesjek and Jalinka. And I have a story to tell about them later on. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was-- it wasn't a rich life, in a sense, comparing it to Canada. You know, there were-- material things, at least what I remember, didn't seem to be as important, although there was sufficient enough food to eat, and so on. But the luxury in homes, we didn't see it as what you see in today's living conditions.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of living accommodation did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in-- in a four-room house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think I shared a bed with a couple of my brothers-- a bedroom, that is. It was a wonderful life. There was an awful lot of love in our family, starting from our parents. My father used to call my mum 'Rivkala.' And he adored her, and it was obvious, you know, because-- and then this was brought down to the rest of us. And so the love that was shown in our house was just running over with-- with caring and consideration. And, of course, I was the baby. So the things that I got away with at home, when I think back, is absolutely astounding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give us an example?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got away with-- I got away with murder. I mean, anything that I did was fine. And-- and I don't ever remember getting a spanking for something that-- that I would do at home. I did get a spanking when the war broke out. And-- and remind me to tell you about it. And that was the only spanking I ever received from my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you come from a religious home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody that lived in our shtetl-- you know, I don't know about the rest of the population. There was one shul that we belonged to. And we were religious, in the sense that, you know, prayers were observed on a daily basis, and kosher was observed. All the yontoyvem were observed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I particularly remember, Friday nights were to welcoming the Shabbat-- what-- what wonders. You know? You know, my mum would start early on Friday morning. And even on Thursday night she was preparing for welcoming the Shabbos.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had guests that would come. And after the meal, particularly-- I refer to those days-- BT-- Before Television. I don't think we needed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: My father was quite-- quite-- how should I put it? He had a little bit of the ham in him. I shouldn't use that word. But he liked-- was reading beautifully. He knew how to read.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember that neighbors would come, you know, after the-- the meal. And he would read from Sholem Aleichem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these Jewish, or Gentile, neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish neighbors, mostly. The-- the Shabbos, the yontoyvem, belonged to us-- belonged to the Jewish people. And-- and we stuck together, in that sense. The stories still echo in my mind. There were wonderful stories about Tevye der milkhiker, you know-- of course you know that is based on that-- and many, many other stories that I remember hearing. The [INAUDIBLE] time, at the beginning, I remember, there was another young fellow that was in the-- you know, a child my age. And we weren't paying attention, at first-- I remember, this one occasion-- and almost, almost got a spanking, there. And, of course, then we started being quiet and absorbed these stories. And I'm glad that-- that we were made to pay attention, because some of them are really wonderful. And, to this day, I get a warm feeling, a glow, when I think of the type of life that existed, in those days. And, of course, I couldn't wait to grow up and to become a teenager, or at least a little older, so my parents would allow me to do what my brothers did. You know, belonging to the various organizations. My sister belonged to Betar, which-- which was a girls' organization. And my brothers belonged to, one of them belonged to the Bund, which, of course, was to the left. I mean, we had all these political organizations, but they were all Zionist organizations. And most of them, except for the Bund, were advocating organizing-- you know, halutzim-- going to the state of Israel, going to Palestine, at the time. Of course, we didn't refer to it as Israel at the time. And I just wish that there was enough wisdom that they had done this, that more Jews would have left Poland before the madness occurred. So when you speak about-- and then, of course, talk about going-- say, Rosh Hashanah, the welcoming of the new year. You know, the warmest memories that I have is standing beside my father in shul. [SIGHING] [SOBBING] I didn't think that this would do this to me. And his tallis, he would-- and, you know, completely-- I was completely [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] hidden, you know, in in his tallis. How wonderfully secure I felt. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I hope I don't do this to you, Fran. Take a moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a wonderful time of-- of being a child. And the security I felt, with this-- with this-- with this gesture of his, you know, and listening to the khazn sing, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there's-- there's no words to describe that kind of a feeling, of having this. And, of course, the solemn time of Yom Kippur, of coming back after-- after the Kol Nidre, and the seriousness of it, and the feeling at home, afterwards. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then the next day, Yom Kippur, my mother would bring a little bag. And she had fruit in there. And, of course, I was too young to to fast, you know, So she would give me a cookie or a fruit. Simple things-- they were so simple, and they're so wonderful. So full of Yiddishkeit.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of woman was your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother lived for for her husband and for us children. I never remember her raising her voice to us. My father was the disciplinarian. He would discipline us. But my mother-- amazing. With her-- her gentleness and her love, no one of us kids ever disobeyed her. I would not be able to sleep if I didn't hug her and give her a kiss before going to bed. I used to-- and she says, oh, don't talk like this because I would say to her, mummy, when are you going to die when I was very young. And I remember that. I must have been no  more than four-- four, maybe five. And she says, well, we'll talk about it later. And I says, well, I love you very much. And if you are going to die, let me know, because I'd like to die the day before you do. I-- I just adored her. And I wasn't the only one. All my-- my brothers, and my sister-- to this day, you know, she misses her mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, ultimately, you know, the way we separated-- maybe it's a good thing, because I loved her more than life itself. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What can you tell me about the school that you attended?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war broke out, I was in grade 2. And I remember that, you know, they had, in the school-- my teacher's name was Rutkuwna, and I can see her, still, you know. She appeared tall, but maybe she was only 5-something. But to me she was a giant. And she was very slim and had blue eyes and blonde hair and had a very, very stern voice, but very gentle with-- with all of us. [? And we wore ?]--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mixed boys and girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mixed boys and girls. And, it's funny I should think about it now. We we we  wore slippers when we went into school, because they had these-- these floors. They had hardwood floors, and they were very, very shiny, and we used to slide on them. We had these wonderful slippers. And I was in grade 2 when the war broke out. They were wonderful years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the
